import { Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";

<Meta title="Docs/Introduction" />

# Amsterdam Design System

This is the library for the user interfaces (UI) of all digital products offered by the City of Amsterdam.
It provides reusable components, patterns, and guidelines for teams working on websites.

It helps everyone work together faster and better, with more time to create actual value.
It makes it easier to align all software we create, purchase and use with our corporate identity.
By using it in all our channels, they appear the same, work similarly, are widely accessible, and inspire trust.

## How does it work

The most concrete products of the Amsterdam Design System are the reusable user interface components, such as buttons, text components, menus, and form fields.
They adhere to the City’s branding: colour, typography and dimensions.
They adapt responsively to different screens and devices and are accessible to everyone.

We make these components available in various libraries so that designers and developers can use them in their work:

### Figma

[Find our Figma Library here](https://www.figma.com/community/file/1530535540611888495/amsterdam-design-system-community-edition).
Various components, text styles and grid styles are ready to use in your designs.
Several others are in progress or being reviewed.
Refer to the introduction and legend on the cover page.

### React

On this website, you will find the React Library and documentation – use the table of contents on the left (on wide windows) or under the ‘Sidebar’ tab (on narrow ones).
These components can be installed and used in your application [via npm](https://www.npmjs.com/search?q=%40amsterdam%2Fdesign-system).

### CSS and Design Tokens

The components are usable without React by applying our classes or design tokens.
If you would like to follow this approach, please let us know.

## We build on the NL Design System

Amsterdam Design System uses the architecture of [NL Design System](https://nldesignsystem.nl/).

Thanks to the NL Design System, the entire government in the Netherlands can work together to provide understandable, user-friendly and accessible online services.
A service that is logical and coherent but not necessarily uniform, because it allows room for the identity of government organizations.

The choice to connect to NL Design System also makes it easier for us to create different libraries.

## Contact us

### Join Teams

If you work for the City of Amsterdam, [register now for the Design System team](https://teams.microsoft.com/l/team/19%3afYKS_RD2n1q4UhguA9jwEJk0A_VjYPO4TiLQjYlG_bo1%40thread.tacv2/conversations?groupId=381b5f11-b342-4a3a-8a78-8b371a90457d&tenantId=72fca1b1-2c2e-4376-a445-294d80196804) on MS Teams.
This way, you can easily stay informed, and we can keep in touch.
The channels have a lot of information you can read, and of course you are more than welcome to participate in threads.

### Watch the demo

For an overview of what’s new, you are welcome to our quarterly demo and four-weekly sprint review.
You can also ask questions and provide feedback here.
You can find the invitation in the ‘Sprint review’ channel in MS Teams.
We record the sessions for later viewing.

### Contribute via Git

[The code repository is on GitHub](http://github.com/Amsterdam/design-system) and is open source.
Your pull requests and issues are welcome.

### Send a message

Do you want to know how the design system can help you? Questions about our working method or suggestions for the roadmap?
Let us know; we are happy to work with you.

Don’t hesitate to get in touch with us by email at [designsystem@amsterdam.nl](designsystem@amsterdam.nl).

The current core team:
Aram Limpens,
Bas Kroese,
Inge de Bondt (lead),
Niels Roozemond,
Ruben Sibon,
and
Vincent Smedinga (lead).

Contributions by:
Bettina Slee,
Hee Chan van der Haar,
Jesper Duinker,
Justin Straver,
Laurens van den Akker,
Mike Alders,
Nico Druif,
Renate Schwarzler,
Rick Zweers,

and
Tanja van der Heide.

<Meta title="Docs/Developer Guide/Getting Started" />

# Getting Started

## Installing

Get the packages your need from npm.
These are all 5 of them:

```sh
npm install @amsterdam/design-system-assets @amsterdam/design-system-css @amsterdam/design-system-react @amsterdam/design-system-react-icons @amsterdam/design-system-tokens
```

Import the components and stylesheets you need, for example:

{/* prettier-ignore */}
```tsx
import "@amsterdam/design-system-assets/font/index.css"
import "@amsterdam/design-system-css/dist/index.css"
import "@amsterdam/design-system-tokens/dist/index.css"
import { Paragraph } from "@amsterdam/design-system-react"

function App() {
  return <Paragraph>Hello, world!</Paragraph>
}

export default App
```

## Reset styles

Add the `ams-body` [class name](/docs/utilities-css-body--docs) to your document’s `<body>` tag.
This removes white space that the browser might add.
In Compact Mode, it also sets the background color to match the Page component seamlessly.

We don’t have a separate, global reset stylesheet.
This follows from our component-based architecture: every component overwrites user agent styles on the elements they render.
You shouldn’t need a reset stylesheet.

## Compact Mode

If you use [Compact Mode](/docs/docs-developer-guide-modes--docs#compact-mode),
import the stylesheets in this order – the compact tokens override the spacious ones.

{/* prettier-ignore */}
```ts
import "@amsterdam/design-system-tokens/dist/index.css"
import "@amsterdam/design-system-tokens/dist/compact.css"
```

## Contributing

You’re welcome to contribute to this project.
Check our [Contributing guidelines](https://github.com/Amsterdam/design-system/blob/develop/CONTRIBUTING.md) when you start.

docs-developer-guide-grid--docs.content:
{/* @license CC0-1.0 */}

import { Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";

<Meta title="Docs/Developer Guide/Grid" />

# Grid

Underlying each page is a responsive grid.
All elements on a page are placed on this grid.

## Design

### 4, 8, or 12 columns

The grid divides the screen into columns of equal width.
The wider the window, the more columns.

In windows up to 600 pixels wide, the grid has 4 columns.
Up to a width of 1160 pixels, it has 8 columns.
At that point and beyond, it has 12 columns.

We refer to these 3 variants as the narrow, medium, and wide grid.

At these breakpoints, only the number of columns changes.
They become narrower or wider, affecting the elements placed on them.
The white spaces between and beside the columns grow at the same rate across the entire spectrum.

### Different densities

The design system supports both public and internal websites.
The two require different layouts.

#### Spacious Mode

The online corporate identity has been designed for simplicity.
Not many options exist for background colour or borders.
Typography and white space remain as tools to keep screens readable.
For public websites, the grid is quite spacious.

The gap between columns uses our `xl` space token, which grows from 36 to 60 pixels in windows between 320 and 1440 pixels wide.

#### Compact Mode

Internal websites often need to present more data on a single screen, which makes large amounts of white space counterproductive.
Therefore, the grid adapts to [Compact Mode](/docs/docs-developer-guide-modes--docs), in which the `xl` space token grows from 16 to 24 pixels.

### Margins left and right

On both sides, the grid reserves white space at the edges of the screen or window.
This uses the `l` space token on the narrow grid (`xl` in Compact Mode), `xl` on medium, and `2xl` on the wide grid.

### Menu bar

In the wide grid, websites have a vertical menu bar on the left side.
This is next to the grid, not inside, and has a maximum width of 128 pixels.

The menu is placed horizontally below the header in the medium and narrow grids.
In this context, the grid takes up the entire width of the window.

### Maximum width

A maximum width for the grid ensures that white space and typography do not grow indefinitely.
This prevents page elements from getting too far apart, keeping text comfortably readable.
The maximum width is 1.440 pixels for public websites, and 1.920 for internal ones.

There is no explicit minimum width.
Even in windows narrower than 320 pixels, to the extent that they occur in practice,
the grid continues to scale to the available width.
For convenience, the width of 320 plays a role in the documentation.

## Dimensions

In practice, these design choices result in the following dimensions in pixels.

### Widths per breakpoint

The columns, gaps, and margins have the following widths at the 3 breakpoints.
Again, between these widths, the widths grow continuously.

#### Spacious

| Breakpoint | From window width | Number of columns | Column width | Gap width | Margin width | Grid width |
| :--------- | ----------------: | ----------------: | -----------: | --------: | -----------: | ---------: |
| narrow     |               320 |                 4 |           41 |        36 |           24 |        272 |
| medium     |               600 |                 8 |         27.8 |        42 |           42 |        516 |
| wide       |              1160 |                12 |         33.9 |        54 |         79.5 |       1001 |

#### Compact

| Breakpoint | From window width | Number of columns | Menu bar width | Column width | Gap width | Margin width | Grid width |
| :--------- | ----------------: | ----------------: | -------------: | -----------: | --------: | -----------: | ---------: |
| narrow     |               320 |                 4 |              0 |           54 |        24 |           16 |        288 |
| medium     |               600 |                 8 |              0 |         44.6 |        27 |           27 |        548 |
| wide       |              1160 |                12 |            108 |         50.1 |        33 |           44 |        964 |

### Not tied to devices

All grid dimensions are based on multiples of 16;
explicitly not on the sizes of specific screens or devices.

We do not want to design our layouts based on the precise dimensions of the latest iPhone or Galaxy,
but instead, make them responsive to the variety of screens they will appear on.
Of course, we have experimented and tested this.
We’ve ultimately chosen values that work well visually.

For the same reason, we do not refer to the 3 grid breakpoints as “mobile, tablet, and desktop” but as “for narrow, medium, and wide” screens or windows.

If you want a bit more guidance,
assume that phones in portrait mode and split-screen windows on tablets use the 4-column grid,
phones in landscape mode and tablets in portrait use the 8-column grid,
and tablets in landscape mode, laptops, and larger screens use the 12-column grid.
The exact number of pixels is not important.

### Reference widths for designers

Nice, all that flexibility.
However, when designing a screen, it is still necessary to choose a specific canvas.
You want to determine how each design behaves on a desktop, a tablet, or a mobile phone.

Therefore, in Figma, we work with 3 reference widths.
On those widths, we develop the screen’s design for the 3 classes of devices.
We know that, in reality, there is much more variation, but we have to draw the line somewhere.
You can find these grids in the Figma Library under ‘Grid styles’.

In these 3 situations, the dimensions are as follows.

#### Spacious

| Device Class | Reference Width | Number of columns | Column width | Gap width | Margin width | Grid width |
| :----------- | --------------: | ----------------: | -----------: | --------: | -----------: | ---------: |
| phone        |             320 |                 4 |           41 |        36 |           24 |        272 |
| tablet       |             880 |                 8 |           56 |        48 |           48 |        784 |
| desktop      |            1440 |                12 |           50 |        60 |           90 |       1260 |

#### Compact

| Device Class | Reference Width | Number of columns | Menu bar width | Column width | Gap width | Margin width | Grid width |
| :----------- | --------------: | ----------------: | -------------: | -----------: | --------: | -----------: | ---------: |
| phone        |             320 |                 4 |              0 |           54 |        24 |           16 |        288 |
| tablet       |             880 |                 8 |              0 |         76.3 |        30 |           30 |        820 |
| desktop      |            1920 |                12 |            108 |          110 |        36 |           48 |       1716 |

### Windows or screens

We intentionally talk about windows, while responsive design often uses screen widths.
Most of the time, it comes down to the same thing, but not always.
It is good to be aware of this.

On a computer screen, the browser window is not necessarily maximized; it can be narrower than the screen.
Responsive design logically assumes the width of the browser window.

On phones and tablets, both concepts often coincide,
although tablets allow placing 2 (windows of) apps side by side on the screen.

## Relationship with text

Layout and text relate in various ways.

### Scaling typography

Like the grid, the [text sizes](/docs/brand-design-tokens-typography--docs) respond to the window width.
This ensures that content looks good on as many window widths and with various personal settings.

### Zooming or enlarged text

People can zoom in on a web page or set a larger text size.
The dimensions of the grid take this into account.
The breakpoints scale with the effective text size.
This way, the amount of columns changes earlier, and large content still fits well.

## Related components

- [Grid](/docs/components-layout-grid--docs)

import { Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";

<Meta title="Docs/Developer Guide/Browser Support" />

# Browser Support

The Amsterdam Design System uses the [Baseline standard](https://web.dev/baseline/) 'Widely available' for browser support. This means we only use features that have been supported in the 4 major browsers (Chrome, Edge, Firefox, and Safari) for at least 30 months.
This is considered a good trade-off between using modern web platform features and ensuring broad compatibility.

We use linters for CSS and JavaScript to enforce this.
These tools help us catch most usages of unsupported features early in the development process, allowing us to maintain our commitment to browser compatibility.

However, there is a limitation: we use a lot of CSS custom properties (CSS variables) in our design system.
Linters cannot check whether the combination of a CSS property that has a CSS variable as its value adheres to the Baseline standard.
For example, a linter can verify that the `grid-template-columns` property is widely supported,
but it cannot determine whether the value assigned to it via a CSS variable is also widely supported.

We carefully test and review our code to minimize the risk of using unsupported values.
If you find any issues related to browser support, please [report them](https://github.com/Amsterdam/design-system/issues) so we can address them promptly.

import { Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";

<Meta title="Docs/Developer Guide/Interactivity" />

# Interactivity

By this, we mean elements with which the user can interact, such as links, buttons and form fields.
They must be clearly recognisable and easily distinguishable from non-interactive elements.
Users must be able to operate them in various ways, such as with a mouse, keyboard and touchscreen.

## Text

The result of activating an interactive element must be predictable, even without reading the surrounding text.

Don’t use generic text fragments like “Click here” or “Read more” for the link’s label.
A more meaningful text helps sighted users scan the page, and screen reader users navigate between links.
It’s also beneficial to search engines.

### Relevant WCAG requirements

- [WCAG 2.4.4](https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/#link-purpose-in-context): it is clear to users what it means to click on a link
- [WCAG 2.4.6](https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/#headings-and-labels): use a label to describe interactive elements
- [WCAG 2.5.3](https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/#label-in-name): the accessible name of an element must contain at least the visible text of the element

## Colour

To indicate that an element is interactive, we use the colour blue.
For example, inline links and the primary button are blue.
We use red for interactive elements that are part of an error message.
Both colours become darker on hover.

Colour should not be the only indicator of interactivity so that people with difficulty perceiving colour differences can still see that an element is interactive.

### Relevant WCAG requirements

- [WCAG 1.4.1](https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/#use-of-color): colour should not be the only way to indicate that something is interactive
- [WCAG 1.4.3](https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/#contrast-minimum): text has sufficient contrast with the background

## Format

Interactive elements are at least 24 by 24 pixels in size, so they are easy to use for a large group of users.

### Relevant WCAG requirements

- [WCAG 2.5.8](https://w3c.github.io/wcag/guidelines/22/#target-size-minimum): interactive elements are at least 24 by 24 pixels in size

## Focus indicator

We use the standard focus indicator of the web browsers.
We only adjust the focus outline with an offset of 2 pixels to increase contrast with dark elements.

When setting up a page, ensure that the interactive elements are in a logical order, usually from top to bottom and from left to right.

### Relevant WCAG requirements

- [WCAG 2.4.3](https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/#focus-order): interactive elements have a logical focus / tabbing order
- [WCAG 2.4.7](https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/#focus-visible): the focus indicator is visible
- [WCAG 2.4.11](https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/#focus-not-obscured-minimum): the focus indicator is not completely hidden by content
- [WCAG 2.4.12](https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/#focus-not-obscured-enhanced): the focus indicator is not partially hidden by content
- [WCAG 2.4.13](https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/#focus-appearance): the focus indicator is at least 2 pixels wide and has sufficient contrast with the element that has focus

## Hover

Hover and focus have different, non-overlapping styles.
As a keyboard user, you must know which element you are activating.
A focus style sufficiently different from the hover visualisation helps achieve that goal.

We use the cursor hand `pointer` on hover on all interactive elements without cursor style (such as text boxes).
Originally, `pointer` was only intended for links, but we consider the association of `pointer` with interactivity to be so strong that we use this cursor more often.

Do not use the `title` attribute.
This attribute has inconsistent browser support, which can cause [accessibility issues](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Global_attributes/title#accessibility_concerns).
Furthermore, interactive elements with the `title` attribute may show a tooltip overlaying the text, making it less readable.

### References

- [Utrecht documentation on focus and hover](https://nl-design-system.github.io/utrecht/storybook//docs/css-link--docs)
- [Specification of cursor pointer.](https://www.w3.org/TR/css-ui-4/#valdef-cursor-pointer) States that you should use a pointer for links and may use it for other interactive elements

## Double click

[Some users tend to double-click interactive elements.](https://design-system.service.gov.uk/components/button/#stop-users-from-accidentally-sending-information-more-than-once) Make sure that this does not lead to problems, for example, by using a loading state and letting the server verify it only uses one instance of the data.

We use the CSS rule `touch-action: manipulation` on all interactive elements to prevent devices from unintentionally zooming in and out when clicking or tapping twice.

{/* @license CC0-1.0 */} import { Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks"; # Language ## Specify the language for the entire document It is mandatory to explicitly indicate in which language the content of your page is. With the correct language set, your website will work better. Screen readers will know what language to use for speech. It is also helpful for search engine optimization (SEO). Specify the language on your website’s `` element. After all, the contents of elements in the ``, such as `title` and `meta`, also contain content in the same language. That is outside the scope of the components of the design system. That is why you have to remember to do this properly yourself. ```html … ``` ## Highlight elements in another language If a specific element is in another language, indicate this there. For example, for a link to an English site or page in a menu: ```html
  • My Amsterdam
  • English site
``` ## Do not specify the region The language tag `nl` is sufficient. Expanding it with the region subtag `nl-NL` is not necessary: > The golden rule when creating language tags is to keep the tag as short as possible. > Avoid region, script or other subtags except where they add useful distinguishing information. > – [W3C Internationalization Activity](https://www.w3.org/International/articles/language-tags/) ## Relevant WCAG requirements - [WCAG 3.1.2](https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/#language-of-parts): indicate in which language non-language text is

<Meta title="Docs/Developer Guide/Modes" />

# Modes

The design system offers several modes to meet different needs.

## Density

The design system supports various kinds of websites.
The optimal layout can differ significantly between them.

Our visual style is quite spacious by default.
This works fine for content-oriented websites, but not so much for websites with a high information density.
We distinguish two levels of spaciousness to cover both needs.

1. **Spacious Mode** for public websites.
   Large type and generous spacing make reading comfortable.
2. **Compact Mode** for internal websites.
   Smaller text and tighter spacing allow showing more data.

Key characteristics of each mode:

|                           |               Spacious Mode               |                    Compact Mode                     |
| :------------------------ | :---------------------------------------: | :-------------------------------------------------: |
| **Audience**              |                  Public                   |                      Internal                       |
| **Canvas background**     | <InlineColorSample color="white" /> white | <InlineColorSample color="neutral-10" /> neutral-10 |
| **Size of body text**     |                    20                     |                         16                          |
| **Largest heading size**  |                    60                     |                         28                          |
| **Medium spacing length** |                    24                     |                         16                          |

(Values in pixels and for desktop.)

import { Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";

<Meta title="Docs/Developer Guide/Release Policy" />

# Release Policy

We aim to publish new releases on a monthly basis.
Breaking changes will occur at most once every 3 months.
Change logs, release notes, and our sprint reviews help you with upgrades.

## Packages

We publish the code libraries of our design system as **5 separate npm packages**:

1. [Assets](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@amsterdam/design-system-assets): our fonts, icons, logos and application icons.
2. [CSS](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@amsterdam/design-system-css): applies our design to all components and utilities.
3. [React](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@amsterdam/design-system-react): implements our component library in React.
4. [React Icons](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@amsterdam/design-system-react-icons): all of our icons as React components.
5. [Tokens](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@amsterdam/design-system-tokens): CSS variables that express our design choices.

## Version numbering

Each package has its **own version number**.
The design system as a whole **does not have** a single overarching version.
Because of cross-dependencies, the CSS, React and tokens packages will often move to a new major version together.

We follow [semantic versioning](https://semver.org) to indicate the impact of changes:

- Patch (x.y.z): fixes and technical improvements, safe to upgrade.
- Minor (x.y): offers new features, check whether you can use them.
- Major (x): breaking changes that may require updates to your code.

## Peer dependencies

Our packages are interconnected through peer dependencies.
The React package relies on the CSS package, while the CSS package depends on the Tokens and Assets packages.
Upgrading one of these packages likely means you will also need to upgrade the others to satisfy the peer dependency version requirements.

Without additional configuration, tools like **Dependabot by GitHub** might create separate pull requests for each package, which could fail due to unmet peer dependency constraints.
Grouping the design system packages into a single update can prevent this issue.
[Their documentation](https://docs.github.com/en/code-security/tutorials/secure-your-dependencies/optimizing-pr-creation-version-updates#grouping-related-dependencies-together) provides details on this.

## Release cadence

Breaking changes are grouped into major releases, which we limit to **once every 3 months**.
Non-breaking changes get **released as needed**, usually at the end of our 4-week sprints, but our setup allows us to publish anytime.

We currently don’t define how long we **support older versions** and backport fixes to them.
We recommend staying up to date with at least each major release.

## Deprecation process

We deprecate public features – React components, CSS classes, assets and design tokens – before removing them, so you have enough time to migrate.

We keep features available until the **second major release** after their deprecation.
All deprecations and removals are noted in the changelogs.
Find migration guidance in release notes, on this website, in JSDoc annotations, or comments in code.

## Deletion process

- Address TypeScript errors that result from changes to names, props and prop values of **React components**.
- Read the changelog to find whether **CSS classes** that you may hook into were changed or removed.
- Do the same for our **CSS custom properties** that you use in your components.
  The Stylelint plugin `stylelint-value-no-unknown-custom-properties` could help here.

## Communication channels

- Packages are published to [npm](https://www.npmjs.com/).
- Use a tool like [Dependabot](https://docs.github.com/en/code-security/getting-started/dependabot-quickstart-guide) to get notified of new versions.
- Each package has its own **changelog file** in [the repository](https://github.com/Amsterdam/design-system).
- **Release notes** are also shared in [Teams](https://teams.microsoft.com/l/team/19%3afYKS_RD2n1q4UhguA9jwEJk0A_VjYPO4TiLQjYlG_bo1%40thread.tacv2/conversations?groupId=381b5f11-b342-4a3a-8a78-8b371a90457d&tenantId=72fca1b1-2c2e-4376-a445-294d80196804) and [Slack](https://datalab-amsterdam.slack.com/archives/CV54XSMSA).
- Ask questions and hear about updates in our **sprint reviews**.
- Any **issues or requests** are welcome on [GitHub](https://github.com/Amsterdam/design-system/issues) or in [Teams](https://teams.microsoft.com/l/team/19%3afYKS_RD2n1q4UhguA9jwEJk0A_VjYPO4TiLQjYlG_bo1%40thread.tacv2/conversations?groupId=381b5f11-b342-4a3a-8a78-8b371a90457d&tenantId=72fca1b1-2c2e-4376-a445-294d80196804).

## Consumer responsibilities

To make the most of the design system and avoid surprises:

- **Review changelogs** before upgrading.
- **Plan for major releases** roughly once every three months.
- **Stay up to date** and upgrade to at least each major release.
- **Join sprint reviews** for early insights or to raise questions.

<Meta title="Docs/Developer Guide/Usability" />

# Usability

## Focus management

In traditional multipage applications (MPAs), navigating to another page resets the focus to the `<body>` element,
or the first element with an `autofocus` attribute if there is one.
For single-page applications (SPAs) this isn’t always done consistently.
For example, Next.js sets the focus on a new page at roughly the same position as it was on the old page.
This can result in an unexpected focus position, e.g. when navigating between pages of a form.

Make sure to correct this focus behaviour in your website.
The simplest and most consistent way to do this is to set focus on the `<body>` element after navigating to another page.
For some websites it might make more sense to set the focus somewhere else,
but be very careful with this.
Your assumptions on how users use the app might not be correct.
Only change the expected focus behaviour when user tests show it’s an improvement,
including for users of screen readers.

import { Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";
import { WebAppIcons } from "../../_components/AppIcons/AppIcons";

<Meta title="Docs/Developer Guide/Web App" />

# Web App

Turn a website into a Progressive Web App (PWA) with a web manifest and app icons.

Here’s how to publish a web app with the proper app names and icons.
This set covers all combinations of common operating systems (Android, iOS, macOS, and Windows) and browsers (Chrome, Safari, Edge, and Firefox).

> There is seperate documentation on [how to add a website icon (a “favicon”)](/docs/brand-assets-favicon--docs).

## How to use

### Install assets

First, install these assets as they come with the [Amsterdam Design System Assets](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@amsterdam/design-system-assets):

```sh
npm i @amsterdam/design-system-assets
```

### Link assets

You can manually [symlink (symbolic link)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Symbolic_link#POSIX_and_Unix-like_operating_systems) and copy the files to the root or a `public` folder next to where the `index.html` is located in your project.
An advantage of symlinking is that it tracks changes to the assets when there are package updates. Symlinks are basically shortcuts to files or directories.

Symlink the app icons and copy the [Web Manifest](https://github.com/Amsterdam/design-system/tree/develop/packages-proprietary/assets/app.manifest):

```sh
# `cd` to the directory where you want to make the symlinks. For example:
cd public

# Symlink the app icons. The symlink file (the last argument) can be named anything you like.
ln -s ../node_modules/@amsterdam/design-system-assets/app-icons app-icons

# Copy the Web Manifest. Don't forget to edit it with the app's name.
cp ../node_modules/@amsterdam/design-system-assets/manifest/app.webmanifest app.webmanifest
```

> Important: the symlink, the last argument in the above command, cannot be a path, but can only be the file or directory name.
> I.e. you need to be in the directory where you want to make the symbolic link.

> Note that undoing a symlink can be done by simply removing the created file or directory link:
>
> ```sh
> rm -r app-icons app.webmanifest
> ```

> Note: not all servers recognize the `.webmanifest` extension.
> See [Using a link element to link to a manifest on W3.org](https://www.w3.org/TR/appmanifest/#using-a-link-element-to-link-to-a-manifest) for more information.

Make sure that the Web Manifest, stating the app's name and referencing the PNG icons, is available in the same location.
Edit the `app.webmanifest` file to include the app's name and the icons you want to use.
For example:

```json
{
  "name": "A maximum of thirty characters",
  "short_name": "Max 12 chars",
  "icons": [
    { "src": "app-icons/icon-192.png", "type": "image/png", "sizes": "192x192" },
    { "src": "app-icons/icon-512.png", "type": "image/png", "sizes": "512x512" }
  ]
}
```

Then link all these assets by placing the following tags in the `<head>`:

```html
<head>
  ...
  <link rel="manifest" href="app.webmanifest" />
  ...
</head>
```

### Submit changes

The symlink(s) and copied and edited files can be committed to the repository.

## Web app icons

<WebAppIcons />

Include `icons` object in `*.webmanifest` (see [How to use](#how-to-use)):

```json
{
  "icons": [
    { "src": "/icon-192.png", "type": "image/png", "sizes": "192x192" },
    { "src": "/icon-512.png", "type": "image/png", "sizes": "512x512" }
  ]
}
```

Link the Web Manifest in the `<head>`:

```html
<link rel="manifest" href="app.webmanifest" />
```

> Note: only include a Web Manifest if you want the website to be installable as a Progressive Web App (PWA).
> Browsers [may prompt the user to install the app](https://web.dev/learn/pwa/installation-prompt) on their device if a manifest is found.

## Further reading

- [_Web App Manifest_ on web.dev](https://web.dev/learn/pwa/web-app-manifest)
- [_Web app manifests: Icons_ on MDN Web Docs](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/Manifest/icons)
- [_How to Favicon in 2024: Six files that fit most needs_ by Andrey Sitnik](https://evilmartians.com/chronicles/how-to-favicon-in-2021-six-files-that-fit-most-needs)

import { Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";
import { AppleTouchIcon, Favicon, SvgIcon } from "../../_components/AppIcons/AppIcons";

<Meta title="Brand/Assets/Favicon" />

# Favicon

Represent the website in bookmarks, on home screens, in syndication and other tools.

Here’s how to display a website icon (a favicon) in the web browser.
This set covers all common browsers (Chrome, Safari, Edge, and Firefox).

> There is seperate documentation on [how to publish a web app with the proper app names and icons](/docs/docs-developer-guide-web-app--docs).

## How to use

### Install assets

First, install these assets as they come with the [Amsterdam Design System Assets](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@amsterdam/design-system-assets):

```sh
npm i @amsterdam/design-system-assets
```

### Link assets

You can manually [symlink (symbolic link)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Symbolic_link#POSIX_and_Unix-like_operating_systems) and copy the files to the root or a `public` folder next to where the `index.html` is located in your project.
An advantage of symlinking is that it tracks changes to the assets when there are package updates. Symlinks are basically shortcuts to files or directories.

Symlinking [the entire favicon directory](https://github.com/Amsterdam/design-system/tree/develop/packages-proprietary/assets/favicon) can be done as follows:

```sh
# `cd` to the directory where you want to make the symlinks. For example:
cd public

# Symlink the icons folder. The symlink file (the last argument) can be named anything you like.
ln -s ../node_modules/@amsterdam/design-system-assets/favicon favicon
```

> Important: the symlink, the last argument in the above command, cannot be a path, but can only be the file or directory name.
> I.e. you need to be in the directory where you want to make the symbolic link.

> Note that undoing a symlink can be done by simply removing the created file or directory link:
>
> ```sh
> rm -r favicon
> ```

Then link all these assets by placing the following tags in the `<head>`:

```html
<head>
  ...
  <link rel="icon" href="favicon/favicon.ico" sizes="16x16 32x32 48x48 64x64" />
  <link rel="icon" href="favicon/icon.svg" type="image/svg+xml" />
  <link rel="apple-touch-icon" href="favicon/apple-touch-icon.png" />
  ...
</head>
```

### Submit Changes

The symlink(s) and edited files can be committed to the repository.

## Examples

### Favicon

<Favicon />

We include a classic icon file with this exact name as a baseline for some browsers and RSS readers.

```html
<link rel="icon" href="favicon.ico" sizes="16x16 32x32 48x48 64x64" />
```

### SVG Icon

<SvgIcon />

Roughly 75% of browsers support SVG favicons, which are more efficient and versatile.

```html
<link rel="icon" type="image/svg+xml" href="icon.svg" />
```

### Apple Touch Icon

<AppleTouchIcon />

An iPhone or iPad uses this image when adding the webpage as a shortcut to your home screen.

```html
<link rel="apple-touch-icon" href="apple-touch-icon.png" />
```

## Further reading

- [_How to Favicon in 2024: Six files that fit most needs_ by Andrey Sitnik](https://evilmartians.com/chronicles/how-to-favicon-in-2021-six-files-that-fit-most-needs)
- [_Definitive edition of “How to Favicon” in 2023_ by Masa Kudamatsu](https://dev.to/masakudamatsu/favicon-nightmare-how-to-maintain-sanity-3al7)

import { Meta, Typeset } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";

<Meta title="Brand/Assets/Font" />

# Font

We have our own font: Amsterdam Sans.

Its clear, open appearance complements the St. Andrew’s Crosses in the logo.
The font is legible both offline and online, so we use it for all communication means.

This sentence shows how all letters and numbers look:

<Typeset
  fontFamily="Amsterdam Sans, Arial, sans-serif"
  fontSizes={[20]}
  fontWeight={400}
  sampleText="Jouw typograaf biedt mij zulke exquise schreven 012345689"
/>

## How to use

When working with a package manager, include `@amsterdam/design-system-assets` in your dependencies.
Then import `@amsterdam/design-system-assets/font/index.css` at the beginning of your stylesheet.

You can also host the font yourself and include it in your application.
Request it through the form below.

If it is impossible to use Amsterdam Sans, use the Arial font, or the device’s generic sans-serif.
Always specify these alternatives if downloading the font for the user fails, or while it is in progress:

```css
.class {
  font-family: "Amsterdam Sans", Arial, sans-serif;
}
```

## Requesting a license

The font is explicitly not freely available.
Amsterdam Sans is licensed exclusively to the City of Amsterdam and amsterdam&partners.
Request the font files through the [Amsterdam Sans request form](https://formulier.amsterdam.nl/thema/huisstijl/amsterdam-sans-aanvragen).

## More information

For additional details about the font, refer to [Typography on Stijlweb](https://amsterdam.nl/stijlweb/basiselementen/typografie/).

import { Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";
import { IconGallery } from "../../_components/IconGallery/IconGallery";
import {
  LearnableIcons,
  BrandIcons,
  DirectionIcons,
  MediaIcons,
  SeverityIcons,
  UniversalIcons,
  FormattingIcons,
  KeyboardKeyIcons,
  DeprecatedIcons,
} from "../../_components/IconGallery/IconGalleryCategories";

<Meta title="Brand/Assets/Icons" />

# Icons

We have designed our own set of icons.

They can only be used on websites where the City of Amsterdam is the main communicator.

We distinguish **functional icons**, which have a specific meaning and can be used without labels, from **general icons**.

Where possible, our icons have both an outlined and a filled variant.

## General icons

We name most of our icons after the objects they picture, like ‘car’, ‘house’, or ‘light bulb’.
This allows using them in various contexts or with slightly different meanings.
All these names are **nouns**.

<IconGallery
  excludeIcons={[
    ...BrandIcons,
    ...DeprecatedIcons,
    ...DirectionIcons,
    ...FormattingIcons,
    ...KeyboardKeyIcons,
    ...LearnableIcons,
    ...MediaIcons,
    ...SeverityIcons,
    ...UniversalIcons,
  ]}
/>

### Direction

Arrows and chevrons indicate direction or movement.
We use ‘forward’ and ‘backward’ to make them easier to use in languages that read from right to left.

<IconGallery icons={DirectionIcons} />

### Severity

The following icons indicate the tone of an Alert or Error Message.
These components use them without a label.

<IconGallery icons={SeverityIcons} />

### Brands

These icons can accompany links to external products or services:

<IconGallery icons={BrandIcons} />

## Functional icons

Some icons are widely recognised and can stand on their own without a label — for example, in an icon button.
Their names are usually **verbs** that describe the associated function.

**Note:** Use these icons only for the action their name represents, and don’t use another icon for such an action.

### Universal

The only icons that are recognized widely enough to be used anywhere without a label:

<IconGallery icons={UniversalIcons} />

### Learnable

In websites that users use so regularly that they can learn the meaning of icons, these can go without labels as well:

<IconGallery icons={LearnableIcons} />

### Media

The following icons can be used without labels in audio or video players:

<IconGallery icons={MediaIcons} />

### Formatting

These icons are used in text editors to indicate formatting options:

<IconGallery icons={FormattingIcons} />

### Keyboard

These icons represent common keyboard keys and can be used without labels:

<IconGallery icons={KeyboardKeyIcons} />

## Deprecated icons

These icons have been removed or renamed.
They will be fully deleted in an upcoming major release, so please update any remaining usages.

<IconGallery icons={DeprecatedIcons} />

## Related components

- [Icon](/docs/components-media-icon--docs)

## Requests

If you need an icon that is not in our set, [check this Figma library](https://www.figma.com/design/SZa5JuGk9AkvAcVKJ9qu6B/Icon-Guidelines?node-id=2006-933) to contact our icon designers.

<Meta title="Brand/Design Tokens/Aspect Ratio" />

# Aspect Ratio

Constrains media content to a supported aspect ratio.

## Design

Media content like images, videos, and maps take up sizable portions of a screen.
Displaying them in a limited set of aspect ratios keeps the visual impression of a page composed.
The default aspect ratio is 16:9.
Content with intrinsic dimensions that do not match an aspect ratio will be cropped.

## Design tokens

<DesignTokensTable path="ams.aspect-ratio" tokens={tokens} />

## Related components

- The [Image](/docs/components-media-image--docs) component has an `aspectRatio` prop that sets one of these values.
- The [Breakout](/docs/components-layout-breakout--docs) layout positions an image with a certain aspect ratio in a Spotlight.
- We provide these aspect ratios as [CSS utility classes](/docs/utilities-css-aspect-ratio--docs).

<Meta title="Brand/Design Tokens/Border" />

# Border

Elements that have a border, outline or underline use one of these widths.

## Design

We use a limited set of border widths for consistency.

## Design tokens

### Spacious Mode

The default border is 2 pixels thick.
One thinner and two thicker options are available.

Some values have a negative equivalent for borders that are drawn inside elements, using a negative offset for a box shadow.

<DesignTokensTable tokens={tokensSpacious} />

### Compact Mode

All borders are a pixel thinner in [Compact Mode](/docs/docs-developer-guide-modes--docs), except for the small width.

<DesignTokensTable tokens={tokensCompact} />

brand-design-tokens-colour--docs.content:
{/* @license CC0-1.0 */}

import { Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";
import { ColorPalette } from "../../_components/ColorPalette/ColorPalette";
import { ColorSample } from "../../_components/ColorSample/ColorSample";
import { InlineColorSample } from "../../_components/InlineColorSample/InlineColorSample";

<Meta title="Brand/Design Tokens/Colour" />

# Colour

Our colour palette is vibrant and crisp.
The main colours are red, blue, black, and white.
They are complemented by two greys and seven supplemental colours.

## Palette

The primary appearance of each colour has been called level 60.
Some colours offer a darker shade at level 80 for hover effects.
We have five neutral colours with white at level 0 and black at 100.

<ColorPalette>
  <ColorPalette.Swatch name="red">
    <ColorPalette.Tile color="#ec0000" level="60" />
    <ColorPalette.Tile color="#b70000" level="80" />
  </ColorPalette.Swatch>
  <ColorPalette.Swatch name="blue">
    <ColorPalette.Tile color="#004699" level="60" />
    <ColorPalette.Tile color="#003677" level="80" />
  </ColorPalette.Swatch>
  <ColorPalette.Swatch name="neutral">
    <ColorPalette.Tile color="#ffffff" level="0" />
    <ColorPalette.Tile color="#e8e8e8" level="10" />
    <ColorPalette.Tile color="#d1d1d1" level="20" />
    <ColorPalette.Tile color="#767676" level="60" />
    <ColorPalette.Tile color="#202020" level="100" />
  </ColorPalette.Swatch>
  <ColorPalette.Swatch name="lime">
    <ColorPalette.Tile color="#bed200" level="60" />
  </ColorPalette.Swatch>
  <ColorPalette.Swatch name="green">
    <ColorPalette.Tile color="#00893c" level="60" />
    <ColorPalette.Tile color="#10552b" level="80" />
  </ColorPalette.Swatch>
  <ColorPalette.Swatch name="orange">
    <ColorPalette.Tile color="#ff9100" level="60" />
  </ColorPalette.Swatch>
  <ColorPalette.Swatch name="azure">
    <ColorPalette.Tile color="#009de6" level="60" />
  </ColorPalette.Swatch>
  <ColorPalette.Swatch name="yellow">
    <ColorPalette.Tile color="#ffe600" level="60" />
  </ColorPalette.Swatch>
  <ColorPalette.Swatch name="magenta">
    <ColorPalette.Tile color="#e50082" level="60" />
  </ColorPalette.Swatch>
  <ColorPalette.Swatch name="purple">
    <ColorPalette.Tile color="#a00078" level="60" />
  </ColorPalette.Swatch>
</ColorPalette>

## Brand design tokens

The term ‘Brand’ represents the highest level in the three-tier token structure of the [NL Design System](https://nldesignsystem.nl/handboek/design-tokens/).

Our brand colour tokens have meaningful (semantic) names instead of generic labels like ‘blue-60’ or ‘red-100’ that other design systems may use.
This approach ensures that the correct colour is used in the appropriate context.
Always use a token for its intended purpose based on its name.
Please reach out to us if you require a different colour token.

### Text

These are the potential foreground colours for text.

| CSS variable                      | Colour      | Example                                                 |
| :-------------------------------- | :---------- | :------------------------------------------------------ |
| `var(--ams-color-text)`           | neutral-100 | <ColorSample value="var(--ams-color-text)" />           |
| `var(--ams-color-text-secondary)` | neutral-60  | <ColorSample value="var(--ams-color-text-secondary)" /> |
| `var(--ams-color-text-inverse)`   | neutral-0   | <ColorSample value="var(--ams-color-text-inverse)" />   |

### Background

We have two background colours.
One for the HTML `<body>` and Page, the other for all other components.
They are the same colour in Spacious Mode, but not in Compact Mode.

#### Spacious Mode

| CSS variable                       | Colour    | Example                         |
| :--------------------------------- | :-------- | :------------------------------ |
| `var(--ams-color-background)`      | neutral-0 | <ColorSample value="#ffffff" /> |
| `var(--ams-color-background-body)` | neutral-0 | <ColorSample value="#ffffff" /> |

#### Compact Mode

The background colour of pages is light grey in Compact Mode.
The cells of the Grid have a white background again.
This is because the limited white space makes it more difficult to distinguish cells from each other.

| CSS variable                       | Colour     | Example                         |
| :--------------------------------- | :--------- | :------------------------------ |
| `var(--ams-color-background)`      | neutral-0  | <ColorSample value="#ffffff" /> |
| `var(--ams-color-background-body)` | neutral-10 | <ColorSample value="#e8e8e8" /> |

### Feedback

Use these colours for positive and negative feedback, warnings, and messages that need attention.
Interactive elements in an invalid state use the ‘invalid interactive’ token, not the ‘error feedback’ token.

| CSS variable                        | Colour    | Example                                                   |
| :---------------------------------- | :-------- | :-------------------------------------------------------- |
| `var(--ams-color-feedback-error)`   | red-60    | <ColorSample value="var(--ams-color-feedback-error)" />   |
| `var(--ams-color-feedback-info)`    | azure-60  | <ColorSample value="var(--ams-color-feedback-info)" />    |
| `var(--ams-color-feedback-success)` | green-60  | <ColorSample value="var(--ams-color-feedback-success)" /> |
| `var(--ams-color-feedback-warning)` | orange-60 | <ColorSample value="var(--ams-color-feedback-warning)" /> |

### Interactive

These colours show elements that the user can interact with, like links, buttons, and form controls.

| CSS variable                                 | Colour      | Example                                                            |
| :------------------------------------------- | :---------- | :----------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `var(--ams-color-interactive)`               | blue-60     | <ColorSample value="var(--ams-color-interactive)" />               |
| `var(--ams-color-interactive-hover)`         | blue-80     | <ColorSample value="var(--ams-color-interactive-hover)" />         |
| `var(--ams-color-interactive-contrast)`      | neutral-100 | <ColorSample value="var(--ams-color-interactive-contrast)" />      |
| `var(--ams-color-interactive-disabled)`      | neutral-60  | <ColorSample value="var(--ams-color-interactive-disabled)" />      |
| `var(--ams-color-interactive-invalid)`       | red-60      | <ColorSample value="var(--ams-color-interactive-invalid)" />       |
| `var(--ams-color-interactive-invalid-hover)` | red-80      | <ColorSample value="var(--ams-color-interactive-invalid-hover)" /> |
| `var(--ams-color-interactive-inverse)`       | neutral-0   | <ColorSample value="var(--ams-color-interactive-inverse)" />       |

### Highlight

Components can use these colours to stand out.

| CSS variable                         | Colour     | Example                                                    |
| :----------------------------------- | :--------- | :--------------------------------------------------------- |
| `var(--ams-color-highlight-azure)`   | azure-60   | <ColorSample value="var(--ams-color-highlight-azure)" />   |
| `var(--ams-color-highlight-green)`   | green-60   | <ColorSample value="var(--ams-color-highlight-green)" />   |
| `var(--ams-color-highlight-lime)`    | lime-60    | <ColorSample value="var(--ams-color-highlight-lime)" />    |
| `var(--ams-color-highlight-magenta)` | magenta-60 | <ColorSample value="var(--ams-color-highlight-magenta)" /> |
| `var(--ams-color-highlight-orange)`  | orange-60  | <ColorSample value="var(--ams-color-highlight-orange)" />  |
| `var(--ams-color-highlight-purple)`  | purple-60  | <ColorSample value="var(--ams-color-highlight-purple)" />  |
| `var(--ams-color-highlight-yellow)`  | yellow-60  | <ColorSample value="var(--ams-color-highlight-yellow)" />  |

### Progress

These colours can be used to indicate the progress of a task.

| CSS variable                          | Colour     | Example                                                     |
| :------------------------------------ | :--------- | :---------------------------------------------------------- |
| `var(--ams-color-progress-current)`   | green-60   | <ColorSample value="var(--ams-color-progress-current)" />   |
| `var(--ams-color-progress-completed)` | green-60   | <ColorSample value="var(--ams-color-progress-completed)" /> |
| `var(--ams-color-progress-upcoming)`  | neutral-60 | <ColorSample value="var(--ams-color-progress-upcoming)" />  |

### Separator

This colour is used to separate components or space.

| CSS variable                 | Colour     | Example                                            |
| :--------------------------- | :--------- | :------------------------------------------------- |
| `var(--ams-color-separator)` | neutral-20 | <ColorSample value="var(--ams-color-separator)" /> |

## Pairing foreground with background colours

### White background

Most of the background of our pages is white.
Both body text and headers are black here, and all kinds of links are blue.

- Text: `var(--ams-color-text)`
- Links: `var(--ams-color-interactive)`

Obviously, these colours are not always effective on other backgrounds.

### Light background

We consider <InlineColorSample color="lime" /> **lime**, <InlineColorSample color="orange" /> **orange** and <InlineColorSample color="yellow" /> **yellow** to be ‘light’ backgrounds.
Black text still provides enough contrast here.
Links on these backgrounds are black as well – this is the **‘contrast’** interactive colour.
The underline and cursor still distinguish links from static text.

- Text: `var(--ams-color-text)`
- Links: `var(--ams-color-interactive-contrast)`

### Dark background

An <InlineColorSample color="azure" /> **azure**, <InlineColorSample color="blue" /> **blue**, <InlineColorSample color="green" /> **green**, <InlineColorSample color="magenta" /> **magenta**, <InlineColorSample color="purple" /> **purple** or <InlineColorSample color="red" /> **red** background is ‘dark’.
On these, both text and links are white – the **‘inverse’** text and interactive colour.

- Text: `var(--ams-color-text-inverse)`
- Links: `var(--ams-color-interactive-inverse)`

# Inputs

Several input components share the same appearance in various aspects.
This set of common tokens maintains their visual consistency.

These tokens are used by
[Date Input](/docs/components-forms-date-input--docs),
[File Input](/docs/components-forms-file-input--docs),
[Password Input](/docs/components-forms-password-input--docs),
[Search Field](/docs/components-forms-search-field--docs),
[Select](/docs/components-forms-select--docs),
[Text Area](/docs/components-forms-text-area--docs),
[Text Input](/docs/components-forms-text-input--docs),
[Time Input](/docs/components-forms-time-input--docs) and
(one of them) by [Button](/docs/components-buttons-button--docs).

## Default

<DesignTokensTable
  exclude={["disabled", "hover", "invalid", "placeholder"]}
  path="ams.inputs"
  showDescriptions
  tokens={tokens}
/>

## Disabled

<DesignTokensTable path="ams.inputs.disabled" showDescriptions tokens={tokens} />

## Hover

<DesignTokensTable path="ams.inputs.hover" showDescriptions tokens={tokens} />

## Invalid

<DesignTokensTable path="ams.inputs.invalid" showDescriptions tokens={tokens} />

## Placeholder

<DesignTokensTable path="ams.inputs.placeholder" showDescriptions tokens={tokens} />

## How to use

Use these tokens if you recreate our React components with different technology, or if you create a new input component.

<Meta title="Brand/Design Tokens/Links" />

# Links

Several link components share the same appearance in various aspects.
This set of common tokens maintains their visual consistency.

## Default

Our default links have the following styles:

<DesignTokensTable exclude={["subtle", "contrast", "inverse"]} path="ams.links" showDescriptions tokens={tokens} />

## Subtle

Subtle links are used in places where the context helps indicate that the text is a link.
For instance, in the Page Header or Pagination components.
As a result, their styling can be less obvious.

<DesignTokensTable path="ams.links.subtle" showDescriptions tokens={tokens} />

## Contrast

Links on light (but not white) backgrounds use the interactive contrast colour.

<DesignTokensTable path="ams.links.contrast" showDescriptions tokens={tokens} />

## Inverse

On dark backgrounds, links have the inverse colour.

<DesignTokensTable path="ams.links.inverse" showDescriptions tokens={tokens} />

## How to use

Use these tokens if you recreate our React components with different technology, or if you create a new link component.

import { Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";
import { SpaceSample } from "../../_components/SpaceSample/SpaceSample";

<Meta title="Brand/Design Tokens/Space" />

# Space

We have a single scale of spacing values for all kinds of whitespace.

## Spacious

Since our typography is fluid, spacing is as well.
Medium space matches the font size of regular body text.
There are two smaller and three larger options available.
The two biggest sizes are mainly used for the grid; they grow faster in wider windows.

| CSS variable           | ≤ 320 | Example                         | ≥ 1440 | Example                          |
| :--------------------- | :--------: | :------------------------------ | :---------: | :------------------------------- |
| `var(--ams-space-xs)`  |     4      | <SpaceSample value="0.25rem" /> |      6      | <SpaceSample value="0.375rem" /> |
| `var(--ams-space-s)`   |     8      | <SpaceSample value="0.5rem" />  |     12      | <SpaceSample value="0.75rem" />  |
| `var(--ams-space-m)`   |     16     | <SpaceSample value="1rem" />    |     24      | <SpaceSample value="1.5rem" />   |
| `var(--ams-space-l)`   |     24     | <SpaceSample value="1.5rem" />  |     36      | <SpaceSample value="2.25rem" />  |
| `var(--ams-space-xl)`  |     36     | <SpaceSample value="2.25rem" /> |     60      | <SpaceSample value="3.75rem" />  |
| `var(--ams-space-2xl)` |     48     | <SpaceSample value="3rem" />    |     90      | <SpaceSample value="5.625rem" /> |

## Compact

While typography in [Compact Mode](/docs/docs-developer-guide-modes--docs) isn't responsive, spacing is.
This helps make better use of small screens.

| CSS variable           | ≤ 320 | Example                         | ≥ 1440 | Example                         |
| :--------------------- | :--------: | :------------------------------ | :---------: | :------------------------------ |
| `var(--ams-space-xs)`  |     4      | <SpaceSample value="0.25rem" /> |      4      | <SpaceSample value="0.25rem" /> |
| `var(--ams-space-s)`   |     8      | <SpaceSample value="0.5rem" />  |      8      | <SpaceSample value="0.5rem" />  |
| `var(--ams-space-m)`   |     12     | <SpaceSample value="0.75rem" /> |     16      | <SpaceSample value="1rem" />    |
| `var(--ams-space-l)`   |     16     | <SpaceSample value="1rem" />    |     24      | <SpaceSample value="1.5rem" />  |
| `var(--ams-space-xl)`  |     24     | <SpaceSample value="1.5rem" />  |     36      | <SpaceSample value="2.25rem" /> |
| `var(--ams-space-2xl)` |     32     | <SpaceSample value="2rem" />    |     48      | <SpaceSample value="3rem" />    |

## Pixels and more

We use pixels in our documentation on sizes to convey a more accurate mental image.
In code, sizes are defined in relative units (like `rem`, `em`, `ex`, `vw`, `vh`, and `ch`) to make components more flexible and accessible.
MDN offers a good overview on these [CSS values and units](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/CSS_Values_and_Units).
The responsive values above are specified using the [clamp function](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/clamp).

import { Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";
import { TypographySample } from "../../_components/TypographySample/TypographySample";

<Meta title="Brand/Design Tokens/Typography" />

# Typography

We have a fluid typography system.
The options for the font family, font size, line height and font weight come in two groups: body text and headings.

## Body text

### Font size

The size of text adjusts to the width of the window for pleasant reading on screens of any size.

| CSS variable                                        | ≤ 320 | Example                                                                                                  | ≥ 1440 | Example                                                                                                  |
| :-------------------------------------------------- | :--------: | :------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | :---------: | :------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `var(--ams-typography-body-text-small-font-size)`   |     16     | <TypographySample fontSize="1rem" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-body-text-small-line-height)" />      |     16      | <TypographySample fontSize="1rem" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-body-text-small-line-height)" />      |
| `var(--ams-typography-body-text-font-size)`         |     18     | <TypographySample fontSize="1.125rem" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-body-text-line-height)" />        |     20      | <TypographySample fontSize="1.25rem" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-body-text-line-height)" />         |
| `var(--ams-typography-body-text-large-font-size)`   |     21     | <TypographySample fontSize="1.3125rem" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-body-text-large-line-height)" /> |     25      | <TypographySample fontSize="1.5625rem" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-body-text-large-line-height)" /> |
| `var(--ams-typography-body-text-x-large-font-size)` |     24     | <TypographySample fontSize="1.5rem" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-body-text-x-large-line-height)" />  |     32      | <TypographySample fontSize="2rem" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-body-text-x-large-line-height)" />    |

### Line height

The line heights have been designed to match the corresponding font size.
Do not combine tokens of different sizes.

| CSS variable                                          | Value |
| :---------------------------------------------------- | :---: |
| `var(--ams-typography-body-text-small-line-height)`   |  1.5  |
| `var(--ams-typography-body-text-line-height)`         |  1.6  |
| `var(--ams-typography-body-text-large-line-height)`   |  1.5  |
| `var(--ams-typography-body-text-x-large-line-height)` |  1.4  |

### Font weight

The font weight can either be regular or bold.

| CSS variable                                       | Value | Example                                                                                                                                                        |
| :------------------------------------------------- | :---: | :------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `var(--ams-typography-body-text-font-weight)`      |  400  | <TypographySample fontSize="1.25rem" fontWeight="var(--ams-typography-body-text-font-weight)" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-body-text-line-height)" />      |
| `var(--ams-typography-body-text-bold-font-weight)` |  800  | <TypographySample fontSize="1.25rem" fontWeight="var(--ams-typography-body-text-bold-font-weight)" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-body-text-line-height)" /> |

## Headings

### Font size

We support five sizes for headings, although levels 1 to 4 are most commonly used.

| CSS variable                                | ≤ 320 | Example                                                                                                                                                   | ≥ 1440 | Example                                                                                                                                                   |
| :------------------------------------------ | :--------: | :-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | :---------: | :-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `var(--ams-typography-heading-1-font-size)` |     32     | <TypographySample fontSize="2rem" fontWeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-font-weight)" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-1-line-height)" />      |     48      | <TypographySample fontSize="3rem" fontWeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-font-weight)" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-1-line-height)" />      |
| `var(--ams-typography-heading-2-font-size)` |     24     | <TypographySample fontSize="1.5rem" fontWeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-font-weight)" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-2-line-height)" />    |     32      | <TypographySample fontSize="2rem" fontWeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-font-weight)" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-2-line-height)" />      |
| `var(--ams-typography-heading-3-font-size)` |     21     | <TypographySample fontSize="1.3125rem" fontWeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-font-weight)" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-3-line-height)" /> |     25      | <TypographySample fontSize="1.5625rem" fontWeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-font-weight)" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-3-line-height)" /> |
| `var(--ams-typography-heading-4-font-size)` |     18     | <TypographySample fontSize="1.125rem" fontWeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-font-weight)" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-4-line-height)" />  |     20      | <TypographySample fontSize="1.25rem" fontWeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-font-weight)" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-4-line-height)" />   |
| `var(--ams-typography-heading-5-font-size)` |     16     | <TypographySample fontSize="1rem" fontWeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-font-weight)" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-5-line-height)" />      |     16      | <TypographySample fontSize="1rem" fontWeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-font-weight)" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-5-line-height)" />      |

### Line height

Headings use smaller line heights than body text.

| CSS variable                                  | Value |
| :-------------------------------------------- | :---: |
| `var(--ams-typography-heading-1-line-height)` |  1.2  |
| `var(--ams-typography-heading-2-line-height)` |  1.3  |
| `var(--ams-typography-heading-3-line-height)` |  1.3  |
| `var(--ams-typography-heading-4-line-height)` |  1.4  |
| `var(--ams-typography-heading-5-line-height)` |  1.4  |

### Font weight

Headings only use the bold font weight.

| CSS variable                                | Value | Example                                                                                                                                                   |
| :------------------------------------------ | :---: | :-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `var(--ams-typography-heading-font-weight)` |  800  | <TypographySample fontSize="1.5625rem" fontWeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-font-weight)" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-3-line-height)" /> |

## Other aspects

### Font family

All text appears in our Amsterdam Sans font.
If it is not available, we use Arial or the generic sans-serif instead.

| CSS variable                        |                Value                |
| :---------------------------------- | :---------------------------------: |
| `var(--ams-typography-font-family)` | 'Amsterdam Sans', Arial, sans-serif |

### Hyphenation

Words that do not fit on a single line will be split with a hyphen and continue on the next line.
We let the browser decide when and how to hyphenate words.
This is why it is important to [set the correct language](/docs/docs-developer-guide-language--docs#specify-the-language-for-the-entire-document) for the website.

| CSS variable                                  | Value |
| :-------------------------------------------- | :---- |
| `var(--ams-typography-hyphenate-limit-chars)` | auto  |

### Underlining

We only underline hyperlinks.
To see how, consult our [tokens for links](/docs/brand-design-tokens-links--docs).

### Letter spacing

Kerning has been optimized in the font file.
We do not adjust the space between glyphs.

## Compact Mode

Our large font sizes for public websites would be counterproductive in internal websites.
They use [Compact Mode](/docs/docs-developer-guide-modes--docs), where both body text and headings are considerably smaller.

### Body text

#### Font size

| CSS variable                                        | ≤ 320 | Example                                                                                                          | ≥ 1440 | Example                                                                                                            |
| :-------------------------------------------------- | :--------: | :--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | :---------: | :----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `var(--ams-typography-body-text-small-font-size)`   |     14     | <TypographySample compact fontSize="0.875rem" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-body-text-small-line-height)" />  |     14      | <TypographySample compact fontSize="0.875rem" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-body-text-small-line-height)" />    |
| `var(--ams-typography-body-text-font-size)`         |     16     | <TypographySample compact fontSize="1rem" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-body-text-line-height)" />            |     16      | <TypographySample compact fontSize="1rem" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-body-text-line-height)" />              |
| `var(--ams-typography-body-text-large-font-size)`   |     18     | <TypographySample compact fontSize="1.125rem" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-body-text-large-line-height)" />  |     19      | <TypographySample compact fontSize="1.1875rem" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-body-text-large-line-height)" />   |
| `var(--ams-typography-body-text-x-large-font-size)` |     20     | <TypographySample compact fontSize="1.25rem" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-body-text-x-large-line-height)" /> |     23      | <TypographySample compact fontSize="1.4375rem" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-body-text-x-large-line-height)" /> |

### Line height

| CSS variable                                          | Value |
| :---------------------------------------------------- | :---: |
| `var(--ams-typography-body-text-small-line-height)`   |  1.4  |
| `var(--ams-typography-body-text-line-height)`         |  1.5  |
| `var(--ams-typography-body-text-large-line-height)`   |  1.4  |
| `var(--ams-typography-body-text-x-large-line-height)` |  1.3  |

### Headings

#### Font size

| CSS variable                                | ≤ 320 | Example                                                                                                                                                          | ≥ 1440 | Example                                                                                                                                                           |
| :------------------------------------------ | :--------: | :--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | :---------: | :---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `var(--ams-typography-heading-1-font-size)` |     24     | <TypographySample compact fontSize="1.5rem" fontWeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-font-weight)" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-1-line-height)" />   |     28      | <TypographySample compact fontSize="1.75rem" fontWeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-font-weight)" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-1-line-height)" />   |
| `var(--ams-typography-heading-2-font-size)` |     20     | <TypographySample compact fontSize="1.25rem" fontWeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-font-weight)" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-2-line-height)" />  |     23      | <TypographySample compact fontSize="1.4375rem" fontWeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-font-weight)" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-2-line-height)" /> |
| `var(--ams-typography-heading-3-font-size)` |     18     | <TypographySample compact fontSize="1.125rem" fontWeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-font-weight)" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-3-line-height)" /> |     19      | <TypographySample compact fontSize="1.1875rem" fontWeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-font-weight)" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-3-line-height)" /> |
| `var(--ams-typography-heading-4-font-size)` |     16     | <TypographySample compact fontSize="1rem" fontWeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-font-weight)" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-4-line-height)" />     |     16      | <TypographySample compact fontSize="1rem" fontWeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-font-weight)" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-4-line-height)" />      |
| `var(--ams-typography-heading-5-font-size)` |     14     | <TypographySample compact fontSize="0.875rem" fontWeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-font-weight)" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-5-line-height)" /> |     14      | <TypographySample compact fontSize="0.875rem" fontWeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-font-weight)" lineHeight="var(--ams-typography-heading-5-line-height)" />  |

### Line height

| CSS variable                                  | Value |
| :-------------------------------------------- | :---: |
| `var(--ams-typography-heading-1-line-height)` |  1.2  |
| `var(--ams-typography-heading-2-line-height)` |  1.3  |
| `var(--ams-typography-heading-3-line-height)` |  1.3  |
| `var(--ams-typography-heading-4-line-height)` |  1.4  |
| `var(--ams-typography-heading-5-line-height)` |  1.4  |

import { Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";

<Meta title="Pages/Introduction" />

# Pages

This section demonstrates how to compose pages with the Design System’s components.
It uses the latest versions of the libraries, without any customizations.
This gives an honest representation of what they can achieve and how they work in the browser.

## Documentation

We offer examples for various page types; see the list in the sidebar.
We separate templates for public websites from those for internal ones.

The ‘Docs’ page for each page explains its design decisions and how to build it.

## Examples

The ‘Default’ example shows the actual page in its entirety.
Some page types have more than one example.

### Flexible content

Reload an example to get new random texts and images.
Use the ‘Control’ tab to see how it looks with your own content.

### Test for accessibility and see HTML output

The ‘Accessibility’ tab offers a report of various automatic tests.
The ‘HTML’ tab displays the entire document as rendered by all components on the page.

### Check how the page would look on various devices

The buttons in the toolbar offer various features, of which the ‘three-layered square’ button replicates the appearance and behaviour of the page on a phone, tablet or desktop.
Press the button with the two arrows to rotate the screen.

### See the page full-screen

Use the ‘Go to full screen’ (or press `alt+F`) or ‘Open canvas in new tab’ buttons to the top right to see the entire page.

pages-public-introduction--docs.content:
{/* @license CC0-1.0 */}

import { Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";

<Meta title="Pages/Public/Introduction" />

# Public websites

The City maintains numerous websites for various target audiences: citizens, entrepreneurs and visitors.
This section presents a number of page types for websites of which Amsterdam is the primary sender, and the intended audience is the general public or a specific part of it.

## Spacious Mode

Public websites must use the default Spacious Mode, which applies generous
[typography](/docs/brand-design-tokens-typography--docs) and [spacing](/docs/brand-design-tokens-space--docs).
This appearance keeps the focus on the content.
It makes information easy to read and helps users find what they need without distractions.

## Page structure

The general structure for a page is:

```tsx
<Page>
  <SkipLink href="#inhoud">Direct naar inhoud</SkipLink>
  <PageHeader>
    <Grid />
  </PageHeader>
  <Grid>
    <Breadcrumb />
  </Grid>
  <Grid as="main" id="inhoud">
    …
  </Grid>
  <PageFooter>
    <PageFooter.Spotlight>
      <Grid />
    </PageFooter.Spotlight>
    <PageFooter.Menu />
  </PageFooter>
</Page>
```

### No Menu

Public websites cannot use the [Menu](/docs/components-navigation-menu--docs) component.
They should offer navigation using the Page Header only.

## How to size the Grid Cells

Everything on the page is wrapped in a [Grid Cell](/docs/components-layout-grid--docs).
Its `span` and `start` props let you define where all sections go on the page.
Do so for all variants of the Grid (narrow, medium, and wide: 4, 8, and 12 columns).

Some typical page sections always use the same values:

### Content page header

The header section contains the page title, publication date, optional metadata, and the lead paragraph.
It is a bit **wider** than the body section and is positioned slightly further **to the left**.

```tsx
<Grid.Cell span={{ narrow: 4, medium: 7, wide: 9 }} start={{ narrow: 1, medium: 1, wide: 2 }} />
```

Breadcrumbs use their own Grid placed directly above the header, using the same cell configuration.

### Content page body

The body section contains the main textual content of the page.
It spans **7 columns** on the wide grid and **6 columns** on the medium-wide grid to maintain an optimal line length for readability.
This also applies to forms.

```tsx
<Grid.Cell span={{ narrow: 4, medium: 6, wide: 7 }} start={{ narrow: 1, medium: 2, wide: 3 }} />
```

### Side-by-side blocks

Link Blocks (a section with a header, paragraph, and standalone link) can be displayed **side by side**, for example within a Spotlight.
To achieve this, use the following configuration.
The `start` prop is not needed here.

```tsx
<Grid.Cell span={{ narrow: 4, medium: 4, wide: 6 }} />
```

On navigation pages, similar sections use Link Lists and are 1 column **narrower** on wide screens.
When rendering them in a loop, the `start` position is only necessary for the odd cells:

```tsx
<Grid.Cell span={{ narrow: 4, medium: 4, wide: 5 }} start={index % 2 ? undefined : { narrow: 1, medium: 1, wide: 2 }} />
```

### Top tasks

Top-task overviews use Cards that contain only a heading and a paragraph, without an image.
On the wide grid, each Card spans 3 columns, fitting **4 per row**.
On the narrow and medium-wide grids, each Card spans 4 columns.
The `start` prop is not needed here.

```tsx
<Grid.Cell span={{ narrow: 4, medium: 4, wide: 3 }} />
```

### Preview cards

Index pages such as news overviews or search results use Cards as well for navigation.
They usually do include the image of the target content.
These layouts require **4 columns** for each Card, which can be achieved with a single spanning value:

```tsx
<Grid.Cell span={4} />
```

## Related components

- [Breadcrumb](/docs/components-navigation-breadcrumb--docs)
- [Grid](/docs/components-layout-grid--docs)
- [Page Footer](/docs/components-containers-page-footer--docs)
- [Page Header](/docs/components-containers-page-header--docs)
- [Page](/docs/components-containers-page--docs)
- [Skip Link](/docs/components-navigation-skip-link--docs)

import { Meta } from "@storybook/addon-docs/blocks";

<Meta title="Pages/Internal/Introduction" />

# Internal websites

The design system also supports internal websites, such as backoffice systems used by our staff.

These typically require higher information density and faster task execution.
The guidelines below describe how to apply the design in these contexts.

## Compact Mode

Internal websites frequently use [Compact Mode](/docs/docs-developer-guide-modes--docs), which applies reduced sizes for [typography](/docs/brand-design-tokens-typography--docs) and [spacing](/docs/brand-design-tokens-space--docs).
This mode increases information density and improves the use of available screen space.

Compact Mode automatically adjusts all components.
As a result, they remain consistent and equally suitable for task-oriented workflows as they are for content-driven public websites.

## Page structure

The general structure for a page is:

```tsx
<Page withMenu>
  <SkipLink className="ams-page__area--skip-link" href="#inhoud">
    Direct naar inhoud
  </SkipLink>
  <PageHeader className="ams-page__area--header" noMenuButtonOnWideWindow>
    <Menu />
  </PageHeader>
  <Menu className="ams-page__area--menu" inWideWindow />
  <main className="ams-page__area--body" id="inhoud">
    <Grid />
  </main>
  <PageFooter className="ams-page__area--footer" />
</Page>
```

### Positioning classes

All children of `Page` have a class name starting with `ams-page__area--` to lay them out correctly on the underlying grid.

### With menu

Internal websites may use a [Menu](/docs/components-navigation-menu--docs) to support navigation across major functional areas or modules.
This pattern helps users move efficiently between different parts of the website while keeping the structure clear and predictable.

The component must be rendered twice.

1. One instance, for narrow and medium windows, sits in the Page Header.
   The user can access it through the menu button.
   Hide the menu button on wide windows.
2. A second Menu goes between the Page Header and the main content.
   This is for wide windows, where the menu is positioned to the side.
   The `inWideWindow` prop ensures that only one of both components is present at all times.

Make sure that both menus have the same items.
